name: Patrick Schmidt
occupation: Kansas State Senator / Former U.S. Navy Intelligence Officer
family: Married to Megan; parents were both public school teachers; sixth generation of Kansas farmers, miners, and teachers
whoTheyAre:
Patrick Schmidt is a Kansas state senator and former U.S. Navy intelligence officer running for the U.S. Senate. He was born in Overland Park and comes from six generations of Kansas farmers, miners, and teachers. Both of his parents were public school teachers. He earned his bachelor's degree from Tufts University in 2013 and went on to serve four and a half years of active duty in the Navy, where he worked as a fleet intelligence watch officer on the USS Ronald Reagan, a targeting analyst with the Joint Special Operations Task Force on the Arabian Peninsula, and an analyst at the Office of Naval Intelligence in Washington, D.C. He recently transitioned to the U.S. Naval Reserve.

Schmidt entered politics after witnessing the January 6 Capitol attack while stationed at the Office of Naval Intelligence. He ran unsuccessfully for the Kansas 2nd Congressional District in 2022, then won a seat in the Kansas State Senate representing District 19 in 2024, defeating Tyler Wible in the general election. He took office in January 2025, making him the only currently serving elected official in the eight-candidate Democratic Senate primary field.

He is married to Megan and lives in the Lawrence-Topeka area, representing Shawnee and Douglas Counties. His campaign frames him as someone who understands both national security and Kansas governance, combining military credentials with state legislative experience. He filed for the U.S. Senate race on February 5, 2026.
theirRecord:
Schmidt's legislative record is short but has a few clear markers. In his first year in the Kansas State Senate, he made property tax relief his top issue and broke with his own party to support a constitutional amendment that would cap annual property-value increases for tax purposes. That cross-party vote is the most visible thing on his state legislative record and could appeal to voters across the political spectrum who are frustrated with rising property taxes.

He also sponsored Senate Resolution 1706 condemning the pardons and commutations of people involved in the January 6 Capitol attack. Schmidt says he was close enough to the Capitol that day to smell the tear gas and bear spray, and his personal experience drives his position on this issue. Before entering the legislature, his public record was military rather than political, so voters have less material to evaluate compared to candidates with longer careers in government.

On the other hand, Schmidt has only been in the state Senate since January 2025, which means he is running for a federal seat after barely a year in state office. He also lost a congressional race in 2022. Some voters may see his quick jump to a U.S. Senate campaign as overly ambitious given his limited time in the legislature. His specific policy positions beyond property taxes and the January 6 resolution are not well documented in public reporting.
whatYouShouldKnow: He is the only currently elected official running in the eight-candidate Democratic Senate primary.
He served four and a half years of active duty in U.S. Navy intelligence, including deployments on the USS Ronald Reagan and with the Joint Special Operations Task Force on the Arabian Peninsula.
He was stationed at the Office of Naval Intelligence in Washington, D.C., during the January 6, 2021, Capitol attack and says he was close enough to smell tear gas and bear spray.
He broke with his own party in the Kansas Senate to support a constitutional amendment capping property-value increases for tax purposes.
He sponsored Senate Resolution 1706 condemning the pardons and commutations of people involved in January 6.
He ran for the Kansas 2nd Congressional District in 2022 and lost.
He won his state Senate seat in November 2024 and took office in January 2025, giving him roughly one year of legislative experience before filing for the U.S. Senate.
He was not named in the NRSC's March 2026 ethics complaint against four other Democratic Senate candidates for failing to file personal financial disclosure forms.
whereTheyWorship: No public church affiliation has been identified for Patrick Schmidt in any campaign materials, news reporting, or official profiles. His campaign and public statements emphasize family values, Kansas roots, and service, but do not name a specific congregation or denomination. His campaign website says he will be guided by the family values he learned from his parents and from generations of Kansans before him, which suggests a values-driven framework without tying it to a particular faith tradition.

quotes.text: We're trying to stop bad stuff.

I was close enough to smell the tear gas and bear spray and to see the people violently assaulting law enforcement officers, and I'm really saddened, especially by what we saw last month at the pardons of people that violently attacked law enforcement officers. And I think that it's a sad day. I think it's a dangerous day for the country.
I would love to see real property tax relief for Kansans.

campaignFinance.narrative: Schmidt's publicly available fundraising data comes from his 2024 Kansas State Senate campaign rather than his 2026 federal Senate race. His July 2024 Kansas filing showed $51,967.24 in total contributions and $1,296.50 in in-kind support. A subsequent filing covering October 25 through December 31, 2024, showed an additional $9,450.58 in total contributions plus $206.34 in unitemized donations. His FEC filing for the U.S. Senate race was made on February 5, 2026, but no federal fundraising totals have appeared in publicly accessible FEC summary data yet. Compared to incumbent Roger Marshall's $4.1 million war chest, Schmidt's financial footprint is modest. His state-level donor list shows a mix of PAC support and smaller individual contributions.
campaignFinance.undisclosed: Several PAC and organizational donors appear in Kansas ethics filings as itemized but with specific dollar amounts not extracted from the public PDFs. Federal fundraising totals for the 2026 Senate campaign are not yet available.
campaignFinance.reportingPeriod: State Senate filings: July 2024 and October-December 2024. Federal Senate FEC filing: February 5, 2026 (no totals reported yet).
